title: Speeding Up AI: Latency vs. Throughput
concept: Latency is the time it takes to get a single prediction, while throughput is the number of predictions processed per second.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main reason we deploy models in production?

options.text: To serve predictions to real users or systems in real-world scenarios
To continue training the model forever on synthetic data
To keep the GPUs running at full capacity without purpose

exercises.explain: Correct! Deploying a model means making its predictions available to users or applications in production.

cards.title: Speed limit for AI
cards.body: When a self-driving car spots a pedestrian, it needs to react in milliseconds. A slow model, no matter how accurate, is dangerous. In production, speed is just as important as accuracy!

cards.title: Latency vs. Throughput
cards.body: Latency is the time elapsed to run inference for a single input (e.g., 15 milliseconds). Throughput is the volume of inputs processed in a given time frame (e.g., 60 frames per second).

cards.title: A single race car vs. a bus
cards.body: Think of latency as a race car: it carries one passenger very fast from A to B (low latency). Think of throughput as a bus: it takes longer to arrive, but carries 50 passengers at once (high throughput).

cards.title: Streaming video feeds
cards.body: A security camera system processes 30 video streams at once. It needs high throughput to handle all feeds simultaneously, even if each frame takes slightly longer to process.

cards.title: Higher accuracy isn't always better
cards.body: You might think the model with the highest test accuracy is always the best choice. In reality, a model that is 1% more accurate but 10 times slower is often unusable in real-time applications.

cards.title: Measuring Inference Performance
cards.body: How we evaluate speed in production:

left.text:
Time per single item
Target: Lowest possible
e.g., 10ms response time

right.text:
Total items per second
Target: Highest possible
e.g., 100 images/sec

exercises.prompt: If a self-driving system needs to react instantly to obstacles, which metric should you prioritize minimizing?
exercises.explain: Lowering latency ensures the system receives predictions as quickly as possible to make immediate decisions.

exercises.prompt: True or False: High throughput always guarantees low latency.
exercises.explain: False. A system can process tasks in large batches to achieve high throughput, but each individual task might experience high latency.

exercises.sentence: If a model takes 20 milliseconds to process one image, its inference ___ is 20ms.
exercises.explain: Latency measures the time taken to complete a single inference request.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a typical throughput measurement for a video processing model?
exercises.explain: Frames per second measures the quantity of data processed in a time unit, which is throughput.

exercises.prompt: You are deploying a face unlock feature on a mobile phone. Users get frustrated if they wait more than 0.2 seconds. Which design choice is best?

options.text: Prioritize a lightweight model with low latency, even if accuracy is slightly lower than a server-sized model.
Use a massive model and batch requests, even if latency is 1.5 seconds per user.
Only process facial unlock features once every hour to maximize throughput.
Discard model optimization since speed does not affect user experience.

exercises.explain: For real-time user-facing features like phone unlocking, low latency is critical to keep the user experience seamless.
